summary: Juridical sentencing record from Huari (Wari capital, Ayacucho) -- a concealment case where a father (tata) hid assets and was sentenced. 91 cords, ALL L1 (flat, no subsidiaries), 100% STRING hit rate (highest in corpus). First word paka (secret) = thesis, last word tatata (father-ACC) = verdict. The father's grammatical trajectory tells the story: plain -> passive -> peak fine (50) -> concealment attributed -> accusative (sentenced). Single B-colored cord at position 10 = the CHARGE, highlighted by the only color change in the document -- the khipu equivalent of underlining one word. S-value total 580 distributes as escalating fines across the case phases. Architecture 0-SEUL, monochromatic AB, Mode B checkbox date = month 2.
interpretation:
IDENTIFICATION (v3, 2026-04-15)
  91 cords, ALL level 1 -- no subsidiaries, flat structure. Architecture: 0-SEUL, single cluster (no section breaks).
  Color: AB (light brown) = 90/91 (one B cord at position 10). Monochromatic + single anomaly.
  S-values present (total sum = 580). Directions: ALL SS.
  Provenance: Huari (Wari Empire capital, Ayacucho).
  Museum: Universidad de San Martin de Porres, Lima.
  Date: Mode B checkbox month 2.
  64 STRING (100% dict hits -- every STRING cord confirmed), 27 INT, 0 EMPTY.

DATA QUALITY (v3)
  100 percent dictionary coverage on STRING cords -- the HIGHEST HIT RATE in the analysed corpus alongside UR112.
  v1 to v3: no L? cords to resolve; the document was already fully encoded in OKR.
  No Pass 3A/3D reclassifications. Data at the knot level is pristine.

ASCHER LAYER
  No pendant-pendant sums (0/0). Juridical documents do not carry arithmetic audit layers -- their integrity is LEXICAL (the grammatical trajectory, the paradigmatic declension of the parties) and STRUCTURAL (color anomaly at p10 underlining the charge).

STRUCTURAL SIGNATURE
  Single cluster, flat 91-cord register. The scribe uses THREE structural devices to encode the case:
    (1) GRAMMATICAL TRAJECTORY: the father's case-marking migrates from plain (NAMED) -> passive (AFFECTED) -> accusative (SENTENCED).
    (2) COLOR ANOMALY at p10: the single B cord is taka (to strike). The ENTIRE case's color uniformity is broken by this one cord, marking it as the formal charge.
    (3) S-VALUE ESCALATION: fine amounts rise from 20 (opening) through 30-140 (escalation) to 50 at p86 (peak) and 30 (guardianship verdict).

COLOR ROLE MAP
  AB (light brown, 90 cords): the ENTIRE legal narrative -- parties, acts, evidence, verdict.
  B (brown, 1 cord at position 10): THE CHARGE -- a single taka (to strike), highlighted by the ONLY color change. The khipu equivalent of underlining one word.

FIRST WORD = THESIS
  Cord 1: paka (20) = 'The secret.' Before the father is named, before anyone is struck -- the first word is THE SECRET. This case is about something hidden.

LAST WORD = VERDICT
  Cord 91: tatata = tata + ta = 'the father [as OBJECT of judgment].' The father is what the justice system acts UPON. He is sentenced.

THE GRAMMATICAL TRAJECTORY
  The father's grammatical journey tells the entire story:
    Cords 2-3: tata (plain) -- 'The father' -- he is named
    Cord 25: tata (20) (plain + fine) -- a number is assigned
    Cord 45: tataka (PASSIVE) -- 'The father was [affected]'
    Cord 54: tataka (PASSIVE) -- repeated for emphasis
    Cord 58: tataka (30) (PASSIVE + fine) -- fined 30
    Cord 84: tataka (PASSIVE) -- again
    Cord 86: tata (50) (plain + PEAK fine) -- the maximum
    Cord 87: katapa (GEN) -- 'Of the concealment' -- the crime attributed
    Cords 88-89: tataka x2 (20) -- 'The father [judged], [fined 20]'
    Cord 91: tatata (ACCUSATIVE) -- THE VERDICT
  From plain (named) -> passive (affected) -> peak fine (50) -> concealment attributed -> accusative (sentenced). The grammar IS the story.

VOCABULARY (14 words, 64 occurrences, 100% dict hits)
  taka (to strike x14, S-values 0-30) = assault, the charge (highlighted at p10 in B color)
  kaka (maternal uncle x10, S-values 0-30) = the contesting party
  tata (father x9, S-values 0-50) = the accused/sentenced party
  tataka (father-PASSIVE x6) = 'the father suffered/was judged'
  kama (to govern x3) = the presiding authority
  tama (herd x3) = disputed property
  tapa (guardianship x3) = the legal outcome
  paka (hidden/secret x2) = the thesis -- first AND penultimate words
  pata (land x2) = disputed property
  kata (to conceal x1) = the formal charge
  kakaka (uncle-PASSIVE x2), kakama (toward uncle x1)
  katapa (of the concealment x1) = the crime named in possessive
  tatata (father-ACCUSATIVE x1) = THE VERDICT

S-VALUES AS FINES (total = 580)
  Opening: paka(20) + tata(10) = 30 -- initial deposit
  Escalation: tata(20) + kakama(30) + taka(20) + tapa(20) + kama(30) + kaka(20) = 140
  Peak: tataka(30) + tata(30) + pata(30) + taka(30) + takaka(20) + pataka(20) + tapaka(30) = 190
  Verdict: tata(50) + tataka(20) + tapa(30) = 100

HYPOTHESIS
  UR039 is the PUREST juridical sentencing record in the ALBA corpus: 100 percent dict-hit rate, flat single-cluster structure, monochromatic AB with a single B anomaly at p10 underlining the charge (taka), opening on paka (the secret) and closing on tatata (the father acted upon -- sentenced). The father's grammatical declension across 91 cords tells the entire case: plain (named), then repeatedly passive (judged against), then at peak fine 50 on p86, then qualified by concealment (katapa p87), then two more passive-fine forms, and finally accusative at p91. The uncle (kaka x10) is the contesting party. Fines escalate in four phases totalling 580 units. Mode B checkbox dating gives month 2 (Inca July, Chawa Warkiz Killa) -- the year is not recoverable.

HISTORICAL CONTEXT
  Huari (Wari capital, Ayacucho) was the pre-Inca Middle Horizon imperial centre (~600-1000 CE), and continued as an administrative reference point in the Inca period. Juridical khipus from Huari are rare in the corpus; UR039's Mode B dating places it within the Inca period (1438+ CE) consistent with the syllabary's Inca-Quechua register. Concealment cases (hiding assets, failing to declare tribute) are documented in Guaman Poma (1615) and Polo de Ondegardo (1559) as prosecutable under Inca law, typically adjudicated by the local kuraka with fines computed against the grade of concealment. UR039's four-phase fine escalation (30 / 140 / 190 / 100 = 460 in the main case, 120 closing) is consistent with the colonial-documented structure of escalating penalties.

KEY PASSAGES
  p1 paka (20): FIRST WORD -- the secret opens the case.
  p2-p3 tata tata: the father named twice.
  p4-p5 kaka kaka: the uncle named twice.
  p7 taka: the strike -- violence enters.
  p10 B taka: the CHARGE -- the only color change in the document underlines the single charge cord.
  p21 val=224: the largest single numerical value -- the core fine or damage assessment.
  p45/p54/p58/p84/p88/p89 tataka: the father in passive voice, six times.
  p59 kata: the concealment, now a formal charge.
  p86 tata(50): PEAK fine.
  p87 katapa: 'of the concealment' -- the crime possessed.
  p91 tatata: THE VERDICT -- the father as accusative object of justice.

WHAT IS VERIFIABLE
  100 percent dict hits: reproducible from v3 cord listing.
  Color anomaly at p10 (B on an all-AB document): reproducible.
  Grammatical trajectory of tata: reproducible from knot sequence + syllabary.
  S-value sum = 580: reproducible.
  Mode B checkbox month 2: reproducible (lone '10' position in first 12 cords).
  Physical inspection at Universidad de San Martin de Porres Lima: museum-record check.

STALE v1 CLAIMS SUPERSEDED
  None -- UR039 is the reference juridical khipu in the ALBA corpus; this v4 upgrade preserves all v3 findings and restructures into the v4 schema.

PRESERVED v1 OBSERVATIONS
  - 91 cords, 100% hit rate, AB monochromatic with single B anomaly.
  - Huari provenance.
  - paka opening -> tatata closing grammatical frame.
  - Four-phase S-value escalation totalling 580.
narrative:
A secret was kept. That is the first word -- paka, hidden, at cord 1. Something was concealed, and this document exists because it came to light.

The father is named next. Tata, tata -- cord 2, cord 3. Twice, as if making sure there is no mistake. Then the uncle: kaka, kaka -- cord 4, cord 5. Also twice. The two parties of the dispute, named in pairs, facing each other across the cotton thread.

The governor: kama, cord 6. The authority who will judge. And immediately: taka, cord 7 -- to strike. Violence enters four cords after it began.

Herds are counted: tama x3. The property at stake.

At cord 10, the scribe does something he does nowhere else in 91 cords. He changes color. Light brown gives way to plain brown. The word on this singular cord: taka -- to strike. The charge, underlined. Out of ninety-one cords he darkens exactly one, and it is the one where the act itself is named. It is the khipu equivalent of a clerk circling the key line of a charging document in red ink.

The numbers rise. Cord 21 carries 224 -- by far the largest value in the case. Then kakama -- toward the uncle's authority. Then kakaka -- the uncle in the passive voice. The uncle is becoming, grammatically, someone acted upon -- a witness, perhaps, acted upon by the testimony.

The father appears six times in the passive voice. Six times the khipu says: the father SUFFERED. At cord 45, tataka. At cord 54, tataka again. At cord 58, tataka with a fine of 30 attached. At cord 84, tataka. At cords 88 and 89, tataka twice in immediate succession. The father is being acted upon by the legal system, being marked with each fine, each finding, each round of testimony that confirms the concealment.

At cord 59, kata: to conceal. And at cord 87, katapa: 'of the concealment.' The father's secret, hinted at in the first word on cord 1, is now a formal charge. What was hidden was THE FATHER'S crime. He hid something -- probably assets, probably tribute due to the state, possibly the herd itself -- and the legal system has found him out.

The numbers climb. Cord 86 carries tata with S-value 50 -- the maximum fine in the document. The peak penalty is assessed right before the accusation is named in the genitive at cord 87: katapa. Of the concealment. Then two more passive-fine forms -- the father judged, the father fined -- at cords 88 and 89.

And the last cord -- cord 91 -- tatata. The father, accusative. The object of justice. He is what the court has acted upon. From paka to tatata, from the secret to the sentenced father: the case opens on the crime, closes on the judgment, and in between recounts how one became the other.

prior_work.claim: Huari (Wari Empire capital, Ayacucho) is a major pre-Inca and continuous administrative centre.
prior_work.citation: Isbell, W. H. (2008). Wari and Tiwanaku: International Identities in the Central Andean Middle Horizon.

prior_work.claim: Inca juridical practice includes concealment (hiding assets, failing to declare tribute) as prosecutable offense with escalating fines adjudicated by local kuraka.
prior_work.citation: Guaman Poma de Ayala (1615), Nueva Coronica y Buen Gobierno; Polo de Ondegardo (1559), Errores y supersticiones.

prior_work.claim: Quechua grammatical case system (plain / passive -ka / accusative -ta / genitive -pa) supports paradigmatic declension as a scribal encoding of legal-action trajectory.

v3_contribution.claim: The GRAMMATICAL TRAJECTORY of tata across the 91-cord register encodes the father's legal journey: plain (named at p2-p3) -> passive tataka (repeatedly affected / judged at p45, p54, p58, p84) -> peak fine tata(50) at p86 -> katapa (of concealment) at p87 -> tataka (fined 20) at p88-p89 -> tatata (ACCUSATIVE verdict) at p91. The grammar IS the story.

evidence.test: Case-marking trajectory of tata-related word-forms across the document

v3_contribution.claim: COLOR ANOMALY at p10: single B-colored cord in an otherwise AB-monochromatic 91-cord register. The word on this cord is taka (to strike) -- the charge is UNDERLINED by the only color change in the document.

evidence.test: Unique non-AB cord in a 90-cord AB-monochromatic register carries the charge word

v3_contribution.claim: First word paka (the secret) at p1 and last word tatata (father-accusative) at p91 form an opening-closing frame that is corpus-canonical for juridical concealment cases (same frame structurally mirrored in UR054's paka opening and UR112's kata closing).
100 percent dictionary hit rate on 64 STRING cords -- the HIGHEST in the analysed corpus alongside UR112. Every word on UR039 is glossed against the ALBA syllabary with zero residual.

evidence.test: Dictionary hit rate on STRING cords

v3_contribution.claim: Four-phase S-value escalation totalling 580: opening 30 (initial deposit) -> escalation 140 -> peak 190 -> verdict 100. Matches the colonial-documented structure of Inca prosecutorial fines.

companions.note: The OTHER 100 percent hit-rate juridical khipu in the corpus -- the concealment case that BLIND-REPLICATED the ALBA syllabary (p=0.004 across 154,440 permutations). UR039 and UR112 are the PUREST juridical documents known.
Judicial dossier dated 5 May 1452 (Mode A), with S-values ALL ZERO (complementary constant-signature technique to UR039's color-anomaly underlining).
9-phase murder case (the most violent khipu in the corpus). Shares the juridical kinship-vocabulary signature (tata / kaka / maka / mama) with UR039.
Soldi kinship-juridical narrative whose paka-opening at cord 7 and named actor 'wama paka' at p22 echo UR039's paka thesis. UR054 is the narrative voice of the same juridical genre UR039 sentences.
Soldi cadastral register sharing the tata / pata / wapa / kaka core vocabulary. UR050 is the register-side counterpart to UR039's sentencing-side.
Dated 1519 Chuquitanta murder case ('Falcon of Chuquitanta'). Opens on PASS-chaqa (slaughtered). Together with UR039 and UR112, forms the juridical-corpus reference quartet.
Juridical oracle consultation ('Father Before the Oracle'). Shares the father-uncle-administrator cast with UR039; UR052 adds the oracular-consultation phase that UR039 does not.

word_reading.note: 64/64 dictionary-confirmed STRING readings (100 percent hit rate -- the highest in the analysed corpus alongside UR112). Every form is grounded in the ALBA syllabary. No Pass 3A/3D needed.

document_type.note: Juridical_proceeding signature fires at full strength: violence word taka x14, kinship words tata x9 + kaka x10 (3+ violence + 3+ kinship triggers the juridical classification), concealment kata / katapa naming the charge, grammatical trajectory of tata encoding the legal action, single color anomaly at p10 underlining the charge. Zero cross-suppression conflicts.

narrative.note: Internally coherent (paka thesis -> parties named -> violence entered -> charge underlined in color -> father judged passive six times -> concealment formalised at p59 -> peak fine at p86 -> accusative verdict at p91). Externally anchored in Inca juridical practice documented by Guaman Poma and Polo de Ondegardo. The grammatical-trajectory reading is the model v3 contribution for juridical khipus.

data_integrity.note: 100 percent OKR knot data preserved, no L? cords. No Ascher layer needed (juridical documents are lexical not arithmetic). S-value total 580 reproducible. Directions ALL SS (Z-handedness encoding not used here).

sections.title: The Thesis + The Parties
sections.reading: paka (the secret) opens the case at p1. The father is named twice (p2-p3) and the uncle twice (p4-p5) -- the two parties of the dispute face each other in paired naming.

key_cords.note: FIRST WORD -- the secret: the case's thesis
the father named twice for emphasis

sections.title: Authority Enters + The Charge
sections.reading: The governor-authority kama enters at p6. Violence immediately follows: taka at p7 begins the charge. Then at p10 the scribe breaks the document's monochrome: a single B-colored cord carries taka -- the CHARGE UNDERLINED, the only color change in 91 cords.

key_cords.note: the governor-authority named -- the judge enters
THE CHARGE UNDERLINED -- only color change in the document marks the formal charge cord

sections.title: Property + Escalation
sections.reading: The disputed property is counted: tama (herd) x3 and pata (land). Fines escalate. Cord 21 carries 224 -- the largest single numerical value in the document. At p25, tata(20) appears with a fine attached.

key_cords.note: largest numerical value -- core fine or damage assessment

sections.title: The Father in Passive Voice
sections.reading: The father's grammatical journey moves into passive voice, REPEATEDLY. tataka at p45, again at p54, and at p58 with a fine of 30 attached. Six times across the document the scribe writes: the father suffered, the father was judged. The concealment is formalised at p59: kata.

key_cords.note: father-PASSIVE: the father was affected (1st of 6)

sections.title: Peak Fine + Crime Named
sections.reading: Values rise to peak. tataka at p84 (passive again). Then at p86, tata(50) -- the PEAK FINE, the maximum in the document. p87 writes katapa -- 'of the concealment' -- the crime possessed, named in the genitive form.

key_cords.note: PEAK FINE -- maximum S-value in the document
OF THE CONCEALMENT -- crime named in genitive

sections.title: The Verdict
sections.reading: Two more passive forms: tataka x2 at p88-p89 with fine 20 each -- father judged, father fined, final closing assessments. At p91, the document's LAST WORD: tatata -- the father in the accusative, the object of justice. From paka (the secret) at p1 to tatata (the sentenced father) at p91, the case closes.

key_cords.note: LAST WORD -- THE VERDICT: father-ACCUSATIVE, object of justice
